risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An allocation list was prepared using computer-generated random numbers and a block size of six." Comment: The study explicitly states that the allocation sequence was generated using computer-generated random numbers, which is a truly random method. Although the block size is reported, the random generation method is also described, so this meets low-risk criteria.

Quote: "An allocation list was prepared using computer-generated random numbers and a block size of six. The manufacturer prepared numbered packs of tablets corresponding to the allocation list." Comment: Although the sequence itself was computer-generated (sequence generation), the explicit use of manufacturer-prepared numbered packs corresponding to the allocation list indicates a concealment mechanism that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study was a randomized, controlled double-blinded community-based trial" ... "Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments." ... "All trial supplement tablets had the same color, form, texture and taste." Comment: The paper explicitly states that participants and study staff were blinded, and the supplements were made indistinguishable, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcomes; this supports low risk for performance bias across the study overall.

Quote: "Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments." Comment: Outcome assessment was largely performed by trained study nurses/field workers (anthropometry and hemoglobin at clinic; diarrhoea during weekly home visits), but the paper explicitly states that investigators and study staff were blinded. The key outcomes here were objective/standardized measurements (electronic digital scale, infantometer, HemoCue hemoglobin) and therefore knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to influence measurement.

Quote: "Information on length Z-scores at two or more occasions was available for 317 (85%) of 373 children enrolled in the study" and "In HIV-infected children mean baseline length Z-score was lower in the group of censored children (-1.84... ) than in those who completed the study (-0.74...) ... The missing data points for HIV-infected children after loss to follow-up were therefore non-ignorable. Furthermore, HIV-infected subjects who left the study had a lower mean CD4 percentage at enrolment..." Comment: Overall attrition was substantial (about 15% missing for growth analyses, more for hemoglobin analyses), and the paper explicitly states that missingness in the HIV-infected cohort was non-ignorable and associated with worse preceding growth and poorer health. This indicates attrition could bias results, even though the authors note no imbalance for HIV-uninfected children.
